segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dark, atmospheric shot of a building entrance, quickly transitioning to a surreal miniature cityscape rendered in cool blue and gray tones. An ominous, low orchestral score begins as a deep, authoritative male voice narrates about "cutthroat competition and self-interest," and how "one firm has managed to survive for more than a century" to "become the symbol of Wall Street." Visually, a large, translucent blue octopus emerges from the fog, its tentacles weaving through the buildings, including a prominent White House model, symbolizing a powerful, pervasive force and control. Golden text 'GOLDMAN SACHS' appears over this misty scene. Another male voice describes the firm as "much admired, much hated, and the best risk managers on Wall Street," followed by a third voice stating it's "clearly a culture that cultivates ruthlessness." A fourth voice adds, "for Goldman to be winners, there had to be losers," all while the intense orchestral music builds. The scene then shifts to a historical reenactment of New York in 1869, with people in period clothing on cobblestone streets, accompanied by the environmental sounds of a horse-drawn carriage and distant city noise. The text 'New York 1869' is displayed. The segment concludes with a close-up of a sepia-toned portrait of an elderly man with a white beard, dressed in a formal suit, as the initial authoritative voice returns to narrate, "In 1886, after making a small fortune from his clothing business..." directly linking the man to the firm's origins.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black-and-white office scene from an earlier era, where several individuals in formal attire, the Office Clerks, are engaged in clerical work amidst stacks of bound documents and wooden desks. A man adjusts a robe on a mannequin while a woman interacts with another man at a desk. A consistent, slightly dramatic orchestral score plays as the narrator's clear voice begins, explaining that 'Jewish immigrant Marcus Goldman decides to get into finance,' seeing a need for small businesses to quickly turn their accounts receivables into cash. The scene transitions to a different office setting where three men, the Office Meeting Group, sit around a table with a vintage telephone and lamp; one man speaks animatedly while another listens intently, and a third man in the background appears to be writing. The narrator continues, detailing how 'bank loans are harder to get for these small businesses,' leading Goldman to offer to buy their short-term debt at a discount and resell it to other investors and bankers at a higher price. The next scene shows a man, the Desk Clerk, in a suit at a large desk, reviewing papers, as another man in a suit approaches and hands him a document. The video then cuts to a modern-day man, the Modern Speaker, in a checkered shirt speaking directly to the camera, with the text 'COMMERCIAL PAPERS' appearing in bold yellow letters, as the narrator explains that 'These short-term loans later became what's called commercial papers.' He adds that 'Around that time, it was the beginning of the investment banking industry,' contrasting it with commercial banks that provide 'guaranteed and safe financing,' while firms like Goldman Sachs provide 'riskier financing,' hence the term 'Investment Banks.' This is followed by a brief shot of a man in a suit and hat at a bank counter, the Bank Teller Scene, interacting with a teller behind bars, visually representing the traditional banking system. The video then returns to the Modern Speaker, who continues his narration, stating that 'Ten years later, Marcus Goldman grows his business to selling around $30 million in commercial papers per year.' The final sequence features a sepia-toned portrait of Marcus Goldman, the Historical Portrait, an elderly man with a white beard and mustache, wearing a formal suit, which is shown in close-up and then placed in a wooden frame on a wooden surface, suggesting reverence or historical significance, as the narrator concludes that 'At the age of 60, Goldman has already surpassed the average life expectancy.' The lighting throughout the historical scenes is dim and dramatic, while the modern scenes are well-lit and neutral, emphasizing a contrast between past and present.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of two framed historical portraits, including a sepia-toned image of an elderly man with a white beard, as a male narrator, accompanied by tense, historical-sounding music, explains the need for the family business to thrive after retirement, leading to the introduction of his son-in-law, Samuel Sachs, as a partner. This transitions to an exterior night view of a stately building prominently displaying the golden text 'GOLDMAN SACHS & CO', while the narrator details how by 1896, Sachs had grown the firm to $1.6 million in capital, shared among five partners. The scene shifts to a black-and-white archival view of four men in suits gathered around a table, engaged in a serious discussion, with one reading a newspaper, as the narrator explains that the business of issuing and trading commercial papers is quickly becoming saturated. This is followed by a high-angle shot of a crowded early 20th-century street, where people gather around a building with a sign reading 'WATCH MYSTERY MAN CLIMB BUILDING AT 2 P.M. TODAY', as the narrator emphasizes the need to find "the next big thing" for growth. The video then cuts to a modern lecture setting at Yale, where Prof. Robert Shiller writes 'Investment Banking Underwriting' on a chalkboard, with the text 'INITIAL PUBLIC OFFERING' appearing on screen, while the narrator explains that by the late 19th century, the "next big thing" was "taking companies public," or "IPOs." A brief overlay of a vintage meeting with a woman presenting a chart appears before returning to Prof. Shiller, who now speaks directly, his voice clear as the background music fades, explaining that if it's the first time shares are issued, it's called an IPO, or "initial public offering." Prof. Shiller continues, elaborating that a private company, owned by just a few individuals, wishing to "go public" would typically approach an investment bank, which would then solve this problem by performing an "underwriting." The final shot is a black-and-white aerial view of a bustling early 20th-century city street, filled with vintage automobiles and pedestrians, as Prof. Shiller concludes his explanation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dark, blue-toned exterior shot of the Lehman Brothers building at night, its windows dark except for one faintly glowing, evoking a somber mood, as a clear male voice narrates financial details about "$30 million of common stocks" and "$10 million of preferred stocks for Sears." The scene transitions to a black screen with bold white text declaring 'THE IPO IS A SUCCESS!', accompanied by a subtle, serious orchestral background score, as the narrator explains how "both firms rake in an unprecedented amount of fees from this deal." Next, the camera shifts to the Goldman Sachs & Co. building, also at night, with a contemplative man in a suit on a bench outside, as the camera slowly zooms in on the golden 'GOLDMAN SACHS & CO' sign. The narrator continues, stating that "as a result of their success, many companies become their clients," and by "1914, Goldman Sachs becomes a prominent investment bank on Wall Street." The scene then cuts to a close-up of a framed portrait of a bald man with a mustache, dressed in formal attire, suggesting a figure of historical significance, as the narrator reveals that "the senior partner Henry Goldman even gets invited to become the designer for the US Federal Reserve System." The video then transitions to black-and-white archival footage, first showing a wide shot of the U.S. Capitol building, followed by a tense meeting of four men in suits around a round table in a vintage office, one speaking while others listen intently, as the narrator explains that "IPOs are a cyclical business" and Goldman Sachs "needs to find a way to reinvest its money." The scene shifts to a crowded, dimly lit social gathering with people in formal 1920s-30s attire mingling and drinking, representing the era of prosperity. The orchestral music fades, replaced by an upbeat, jazzy tune, as the narrator declares, "The timing is flawless." The final shot is a modern-day speaker in a dark suit and pink tie, gesturing emphatically in front of a blurred background of financial data, as an enthusiastic male voice proclaims, "The Roaring Twenties is one of the greatest bull markets in the history of America," followed by another exclaiming, "It was a boom like we've never had!"

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a contemporary financial speaker, identified as Jim Cramer, host of CNBC’s Mad Money, speaking directly to the camera in a modern studio. He reflects on a past era, stating there "was a belief that we were in the Emerald City," and describes the "roaring 20s" as a "go-go" period where "everybody was optimistic, markets were soaring," and it was "so easy for anybody to go get out a loan and then go speculate." This commentary overlays black-and-white archival footage of a bustling stock exchange floor, with traders shouting and gesturing amidst flashing numbers like '214' and '215'. The scene transitions to Neel Kashkari, President of the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis, who, in a formal office, introduces Goldman Sachs, explaining that "As an opportunist, Goldman Sachs intends to fully capitalize on it." A subtle click/tap sound accompanies the subsequent visual of a stylized, dark blue animated building labeled 'GOLDMAN SACHS & CO' at night. As the camera pans to the 'GOLDMAN SACHS TRADING CO.' sign with a '$1M -> $10M' text overlay, Kashkari details how it "forms a trading corporation by putting down $1 million in capital," then "immediately sells more shares to the public at 10 times the price," resulting in the corporation raising "$93 million" and their "original investment" becoming "worth almost $10 million." The narrative then shifts to a modern speaker in a checkered shirt, who explains that "Today the entity is more or less like the SPACs. Special Purpose Acquisition Corporations." He asserts that "the essence of finance hasn't really changed," even as he describes how "After making money virtually from thin air, Goldman Sachs starts more investment trusts, simply because there are people in the market willing to pay for them," before trailing off with "This is not unlike." This explanation is intercut with more black-and-white historical footage of a stock exchange ticker board displaying 'DOW JONES AVERAGES' and 'TODAY'S SALES', before returning to the modern speaker.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Modern Speaker, a man in a checkered shirt seated in a modern office, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, his clear narrative voice drawing parallels between past economic bubbles like the 'dot.com bubble' and 'cryptocurrency mania' to the 'Roaring 20s' where 'investment trusts' were created 'out of thin air.' The scene transitions to a black-and-white historical montage, accompanied by an older, formal, and slightly crackly archival male voice proclaiming a 'final triumph over poverty.' However, the Modern Speaker interjects that 'nothing good lasts forever,' as visuals show a large crowd gathered before a building advertising a 'MYSTERY MAN CLIMB', followed by a woman adjusting her wide-brimmed hat, and a chaotic scrambling crowd. Discarded paper ribbons lie on the ground, hinting at a failed event. The Modern Speaker then declares the fateful date, 'October 29th, 1929,' as it appears over a cityscape, marking the stock market crash. A different archival male voice, sounding like a news announcer, details the devastating aftermath: 'inactive manpower... drifting helplessly in search of work,' 'marriages off 22%,' 'birth rate down sharply,' and a 'suicide rate 40% higher.' Visually, a truck loaded with goods and a destitute group with children sitting on the ground underscore the economic hardship. The Modern Speaker emphasizes that 'the crash is the worst America has seen,' as a close-up of The New York Times displays headlines about '90% DEPRECIATION' and 'Goldman Sachs Trading Off $289,566,330.' He concludes by stating that 'Goldman Sachs Trading Corporation is reduced almost to nothing,' with 'hundreds of millions of dollars evaporated,' and that the 'partnership of Goldman Sachs lost $13 million of its investment,' which is visually represented by men in suits in a black-and-white office, exchanging documents, with a stark '-$13M' overlay.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with an ominous, low-frequency hum as a black-and-white, high-angle shot reveals a dense crowd in early 20th-century attire gathered around a pole with a 'MORNINGTON' sign, setting a scene of public event or rally. A male narrator explains that "Within a year, hundreds of investment trusts go bust, taking down investors and investment banks with them." The scene cuts to a black screen displaying 'BUT GOLDMAN SACHS SURVIVED.', emphasizing resilience amid crisis. The hum continues as the narrator states, "Goldman Sachs manages to survive the crash, and its clients surprisingly stay with the firm. All thanks to one man. Sidney Weinberg." Visually, an elegant, dimly lit interior shows a bald, well-dressed man conversing with two standing men, followed by a close-up of a framed black-and-white portrait of a man in a pinstripe suit, glasses, and pocket square, which the audio identifies as Sidney Weinberg. The scene shifts to a modern-day street view of Red Hook, Brooklyn, with a 'RED HOOK LOBSTER POUND' sign and a passing van, overlaid with 'Red Hook, Brooklyn' text, while the hum persists. A young boy in a cap and apron is then seen in a kitchen, receiving a piece of paper from an older man, visually suggesting mentorship, as the narrator details Sidney Weinberg's early life: "Sidney Weinberg is raised in a poor Brooklyn household. He's the third of 11 children. To support the family, he's forced to quit high school early. But even as a young man, Sidney shows a willingness to work hard and the ability to network." The portrait of the man, now with a nameplate reading 'PETER' (visually conflicting with the audio's "Sidney Weinberg"), reappears. A low-angle shot of someone mopping a wooden floor in a modern space visually represents Sidney's humble beginnings, as the narrator confirms, "Started as a janitor at Goldman Sachs. He hustled his way to the very top of the company in a period of 15 years." Finally, Charles D. Ellis, identified by on-screen text as an author, speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, commenting on the subject: "He had a tremendous capacity for connectivity with other people. It was impossible to not like..." The portrait of the man (Sidney Weinberg/Peter) is shown again, maintaining its central role as the hum fades.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with archival black-and-white footage of early 20th-century automobiles, including Model Ts, driving out of a factory, as an unidentified male voice states Henry Ford's vision for "a car in every driveway." This historical industrial setting transitions to a sweeping view of a massive industrial complex with smokestacks billowing smoke and a dramatic close-up of molten metal being poured, while the narrator explains that by 1956, Ford was the second largest car maker in America, and its IPO would make any investment bank a fortune. A framed portrait of Henry Ford is shown in a dimly lit brick room, followed by Douglas Brinkley, an author, who appears in a sophisticated room and audibly explains that Henry Ford "never trusted Wall Street," viewing it as "gambling" and a way "to rip off the folks." The narrative then highlights Ford's controversial views with a historical portrait of him alongside a quote from 'The Dearborn Independent' stating, 'The Jew is a race that has no civilization to point to, no aspiring religion, no great achievement in any realm.' Donny Deutsch, former Chairman of Deutsch Inc., appears next in a modern, softly lit room, audibly stating that Ford "was a great man but not a good man," and "was really a devout anti-Semite," emphasizing that "we can never mistake greatness in business for being a great person." The video shows black-and-white portraits of men in suits and a historical photograph of Walter S. Gresham, as the narrator mentions JP Morgan Jr. failing to take Ford public. A bold title card declares, 'FORD WAS THE BIGGEST IPO IN HISTORY,' as the narrator notes that nine years after Henry Ford died, his grandson, Henry Ford II, was a "vastly different man," and for Sidney Weinberg, this was "his chance to cash in." The segment concludes with Bill Cohan, an author, appearing on a laptop screen in a modern office, as upbeat, slightly tense industrial-sounding music plays, and the narrator mentions a "big breakthrough."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment begins with an upbeat, slightly dramatic background score as a close-up of a yellowed newspaper clipping, titled 'Ford Stock Set at $64.50; First Deals Drive Up Price' and featuring Louis P. Singer, details the Ford Motor Company's initial public offering. A male narrator explains that following the "IPO of GE and Ford Motors," Goldman Sachs became "one of the most profitable investment banks on Wall Street." The scene transitions to a modern exterior shot of the 'Goldman Sachs' logo illuminated at night, reflecting the firm's enduring presence, as the narrator adds, "But to them, it's never enough." The camera then cuts to an interview with Sumner Redstone, identified as 'MAJORITY OWNER, VIACOM & CBS,' who, in a clear, slightly gravelly, and emphatic voice, speaks directly to the camera in a dimly lit, formal setting, asserting, "They don't think in terms of money. They think in terms of winning... Your objective should be to win. To win, win, win all the time. Not sometimes, every time." The video returns to the Goldman Sachs building, followed by a black-and-white archival shot of a crowded, bustling trading floor, labeled 'BLOCK TRADING,' depicting intense financial activity. The narrator explains that "One Goldman Sachs trader has struck gold with a trading strategy called Block Trading." The sequence continues with a close-up of a vintage rotary telephone, then a low-angle shot of a statue of a man on horseback in front of a grand, historic building. The narrator then identifies the trader as "Gus Levy," describing him as "Born in a middle-class household in May 1910 in New Orleans." The final shot shows a framed portrait of a man in a suit and tie placed on a wooden kitchen counter, with a stove and dishes in the background, suggesting a personal or familial connection to the newly introduced figure of Gus Levy.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a framed portrait of a man in a suit and tie, set on a wooden surface, as a male narrator begins to speak about making 'big bucks in New York' over a light, dramatic instrumental background score. This transitions to grainy, black-and-white archival footage of a chaotic trading floor, where men in suits scramble and gesture, illustrating the intense financial activity as the narrator explains how 'Levy' (Gus Levy) became a millionaire and a respected partner at Goldman Sachs through 'calculated risk-taking.' Bold white text 'BLOCK TRADE' then appears, followed by an animated graphic depicting a block trade with stock certificates, a building, cash, and a handshake, visually representing 'block trading,' which the narrator identifies as Levy's specialty and a form of arbitrage. A modern speaker, a man in a checkered shirt, then appears in a dimly lit office, gesturing as he explains how Levy learned to negotiate private prices at a discount for large blocks of shares, reselling them gradually for profit, capitalizing on market inefficiencies. The scene shifts to a yellowed newspaper clipping featuring Sidney Weinberg, with the headline 'Mr. Wall Street to Mark His 60th Year at Goldman, Sachs,' as the narrator details Weinberg's approaching retirement and Gus Levy becoming his 'heir apparent,' marking the first time a trader became the boss at Goldman Sachs. This is followed by a sepia-toned group photo of five men in suits, standing authoritatively in front of bookshelves. Finally, Leon Cooperman, a hedge fund manager, is seen speaking to an audience in a modern conference room, his distinct voice stating, 'This guy was a shaker-maker doer. Gus was in there...'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a framed black-and-white portrait of a man in a suit and tie, placed on a wooden surface beside a vintage rotary telephone, as an unseen male narrator's voice begins, describing someone whose "life was a business," working "around the clock." The scene transitions to another framed portrait of a bald man in a suit, displayed on a dark wooden wall with 'FINANCE MAGAZINE OF MONEY' text, while the unseen narrator continues, explaining how "Gus Levy" drastically grew the trading business, making it a major revenue source. As the camera pans up the exterior of a modern glass skyscraper at dusk, the unseen narrator details how "Goldman Sachs" issuing more securities and engaging in larger-scale trading made the firm more vulnerable. A man in a checkered shirt then appears, seated in a modern office, speaking directly to the camera with a calm demeanor, stating that for finance companies, "it doesn't matter how big they get, they're often just one mistake away from a total collapse." The unseen narrator's voice returns, declaring "Goldman Sachs is back to make that mistake," accompanied by a dramatic musical sting. The scene shifts to an exterior shot of a building entrance marked '200 WEST STRE', followed by a bustling Penn Station entrance, as a train horn sounds and train movement is heard. Vintage footage then shows a Penn Central train, number '3107', pulling a teal freight car through a snowy landscape, with the unseen narrator explaining that "the Pennsylvania Railroad acquires New York Central Railroad in 1968," becoming "the country's largest railroad company, called Penn Central." The train sounds continue as black-and-white archival footage of a crowded street appears, overlaid with bold yellow text '$1.2B', which the unseen narrator links to the company already having "$1.2 billion in debt." The segment concludes with a scene of a man at a bank counter with '$100M' text appearing, as the unseen narrator adds that the company "still needs more to operate" and "hires Goldman Sachs to issue..." with increasingly intense background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black-and-white scene of a formal dinner, where men in suits are seated around a table, engaged in conversation, with the bold yellow text '$100M' superimposed. A clear male voice explains this represents "another $100 million of commercial papers" with a high rating, underwritten by Goldman Sachs, leading investors to eagerly buy Penn Central's papers, believing them to be a safe investment. The scene dramatically transitions to a dark screen with the white text 'BUT THEY WERE WRONG.' This is followed by an aerial view of a bustling rail yard with multiple freight trains and tracks, accompanied by a low, ominous background hum. The narrative then shifts to a close-up of a yellowed newspaper clipping from The New York Times dated May 1, 1974, with the headline 'S.E.C. Files Penn Central Fraud Suit.' A distinct, older-sounding male voice reports that despite the bankruptcy trustees' efforts, Penn Central failed to generate sufficient revenues, leading to the deterioration of its track and equipment. The scene cuts to a modern speaker, a man in a checkered shirt, seated in an office, who explains that after Penn Central's bankruptcy, investors were nearly wiped out and immediately began suing Goldman Sachs for misrepresentation. The video then returns to another newspaper clipping from The New York Times, dated October 10, 1974, with the headline 'GOLDMAN, SACHS LOSES RAIL CASE,' as the modern speaker elaborates on the irony: a national credit office gave the debt a high rating *because* Goldman Sachs was its underwriter, and Goldman Sachs then pushed the company to issue more debt due to these high ratings.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a yellowed New York Times newspaper clipping, headlining 'GOLDMAN, SACHS LOSES RAIL CASE,' visually introducing the firm's legal defeat. A black screen then provides textual context: 'But at the time of the settlements, Goldman said the payments were covered by insurance.' Throughout, a low, suspenseful drone music plays as a Modern Speaker, a man in a checkered shirt, narrates that Goldman Sachs was ordered to pay 20 cents on the dollar for Penn Central's commercial papers. He remarks, "That's interesting, isn't it? It's almost as if Goldman Sachs knew something was going to happen beforehand," adding that "the Penn Central scandal is just a sign of things to come for Goldman Sachs." As the music intensifies, he explains that "as the economic conditions worsened in the 1970s, more securities Goldman Sachs issued are going bad," leading to the firm being "repeatedly forced to pay millions of dollars in settlements to companies like Getty Oil and University Health Foundation." Visually, a nighttime exterior of a modern building with a van driving past, and a black-and-white archival shot of a protest crowd holding signs like 'Fidelifacts Spies on Human Lives' and 'I Lost My Job Through the New York Times' punctuate the narrative of financial turmoil and public unrest. The speaker concludes, "But none of this prepares Goldman Sachs for the tragedy that is about to unfold," as the music becomes more ominous. The video then shows a close-up of a judge’s gavel being struck, accompanied by the distinct sound of a gavel strike and metallic clanking, symbolizing legal judgment. This is followed by a daytime shot of a Getty gas station, visually linking to the mentioned settlements, and a selective color shot of American flags waving in front of a classical building. The segment concludes with a dimly lit kitchen scene where a man in a light-colored shirt with a plaid scarf is focused on cooking, stirring a pot, offering a personal contrast to the preceding institutional and public events.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a soft, melancholic piano melody as a man, the Boy in Kitchen, is seen cooking in a dimly lit kitchen, stirring a pot amidst fresh ingredients like lemons, tomatoes, and garlic, accompanied by a subtle clinking sound. The scene abruptly cuts to black, then transitions to a black-and-white historical clip of a man in a suit speaking at a podium with microphones, evoking a formal address. A male narrator then introduces Gus Levy, stating, "For a while, Gus Levy seemed invincible," as a close-up of a yellowed newspaper clipping with the headline 'Rockefeller Eulogizes Gustave Levy Of Wall Street as 'Fabulous Friend'' is shown, featuring Gus Levy's portrait and details of his life. The narrator continues, explaining how Gus Levy "transformed Goldman Sachs from a boutique investment banking house to an elite institution," while an image of Gus Levy with his years '1910–1976' is displayed on a large screen against a grand building, followed by a sepia-toned photograph of him smiling with two other men. The narrative takes a somber turn as the narrator reveals, "But in recent years, the stress may have caught up to him. He dies from a stroke during a business trip," and that "It's a shock to everyone at the firm, as Gus Levy never even left any succession plan." The piano music continues, becoming slightly more intense, as the narrator sets up the next development: "But people at Goldman Sachs know there is one man who's capable for the job. And he may just be the guy to turn Goldman Sachs around." The narrator then names "John Whitehead," coinciding with a dramatic black-and-white naval battle scene featuring warships firing and smoke, punctuated by a loud explosion sound effect. A portrait of a man with light hair is overlaid on the battle, and the narrator identifies him, stating, "Harvard educated, John Whitehead was also a Navy commander during World War II and was a part of the D-Day," before the audio cuts off. A close-up of a gray-haired man with intense eyes looking off-camera is briefly shown.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dramatic, sepia-toned montage of military action, showing soldiers in a tank, charging through water, and naval vessels advancing across the ocean. An authoritative male voice states that business needs to be conducted "the way has been" and that "the firm needs to operate like the military, where everyone must follow a code of conduct." This intense sequence transitions to a calm, well-lit office environment where Anthony Scaramucci, in a suit, speaks directly to the camera. An older, reflective male voice then wonders how new hires could be "indoctrinated with the principles of Goldman Sachs" and the expected conduct, as a close-up of a document titled 'Our Business Principles' is shown, clipped to a yellow folder with visible text emphasizing client interests, teamwork, and ethical conduct. The voice continues, explaining how he "wrote down what turned out to be these principles" on a yellow pad on a Sunday afternoon. The scene cuts to a black-and-white office setting where a man writes on a whiteboard while another listens intently, while the authoritative male voice from the beginning returns, mentioning that "Whitehead also starts to standardize the investment banking business at Goldman, making sure the employees put their clients interest first." The narrative returns to Scaramucci, now in a different office setting with warm lighting and wooden furnishings, continuing his commentary with an energetic, conversational tone. He recounts a story about arriving at a meeting at 5 PM on a Friday before a holiday weekend, waiting for five hours for someone who showed up at 10 o'clock, and the lesson being about "waiting for people that are more important than you." Interspersed are brief, stylized shots of a modern office lounge with a vibrant yellow floral arrangement, and a dimly lit airport terminal with travelers and luggage. The segment concludes with Scaramucci speaking again, his gestures emphasizing his points, reinforcing the theme of corporate culture and leadership.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a man in a suit, transitioning to a full shot of him seated at a desk in a vintage office, surrounded by an old computer, framed photos, and stacks of papers. A subtle, dramatic corporate synth track begins as a male voice (the Modern Speaker) discusses "Whitehead's" leadership, which "not only makes Goldman Sachs more profitable" but also "restores the trustworthiness in the minds of their clients," noting the "timing again, is impeccable." The scene shifts to a modern office hallway where a framed portrait of an older man in a suit with a purple tie hangs beside the 'GOLDMAN SACHS' logo, emphasizing corporate legacy, as the speaker continues, stating "Whitehead knows, to win in the business of investment banking, Goldman Sachs must provide the highest quality of service." The narrative then moves to the Modern Speaker, a man in a checkered shirt, who speaks directly to the camera in a dimly lit office, transitioning to thank "Kamikoto for sponsoring this video." He introduces the 'kamikoto/神等' brand of "high quality Japanese steel knives," explaining they are "using traditional techniques from Japan," "sourced entirely from mills in Japan," and "each blade is handcrafted using techniques that have been honed and perfected by generations of knifesmits." The camera focuses on a wooden box containing three sleek knives with black handles and silver blades, displayed with a 'Certificate of Authenticity.' The video then shows the meticulous process of knife-making, including sparks flying during metalwork and a hand polishing a blade in water, visually reinforcing the speaker's description of craftsmanship. The Modern Speaker reappears, gesturing as he speaks, before the scene cuts to a close-up of the knife box, highlighting the brand name engraved on the wood. He shares his personal experience, stating, "I have always respected the craftsmanship of a lot of Japanese products," and upon receiving the "Kamikoto knife set," he found them "so well made" that he was "hesitant of using them to avoid leaving stains on them." Finally, the video shows hands using one of the knives to precisely chop celery and carrots on a wooden cutting board, demonstrating the knives' sharpness and utility in a kitchen setting, while the corporate synth music continues subtly in the background.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a framed portrait of a man in a tuxedo, placed on a wooden surface beside a vintage black rotary telephone. A large yellow text overlay '$400M' appears as the narrator explains that "Under the leadership of John Whitehead in 1983, Goldman Sachs makes $400 million dollars in profit," a "60% increase from the previous year." The camera then transitions to a sepia-toned photograph of two men in suits engaged in conversation, set against classical architecture, as the narrator continues that "at the height of his power, Whitehead retires from Goldman" to become Deputy Secretary of State under President Ronald Reagan, passing the mantle to "Steve Freeman and Robert Rubin." This is followed by a close-up of a smiling man with gray hair, then a modern office environment with the 'GOLDMAN SACHS' logo on the wall, where a framed portrait of a serious man in a suit and glasses sits on a desk. The camera zooms in on this portrait as the narrator details how "Ruben built the options trading business at Goldman from scratch into a major profit engine for the firm," now having Goldman "under his control." A dark, moody shot then shows Rudy Giuliani standing behind a podium with a microphone, accompanied by ominous background music, as the narrator warns that "an unlikely adversary is coming after them." The video cuts to a newspaper clipping with the headline 'Rising Targets of Insider Trading Inquiry', featuring Robert M. Freeman, as the narrator reveals that "In 1987, Goldman Sachs senior partner Robert Freeman is under arrest for insider trading." The final shot displays another newspaper article titled 'Storer Will Now Take A Buyout To Go Private In $2.1 Billion Kohlberg Deal', featuring Timothy L. Tabor, while the narrator mentions that "A few years back, Goldman Sachs was the underwriter for KKR."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Mr. Bob Rubin, a man in a dark suit and patterned tie, seated thoughtfully in an orange armchair, clasping his hands as a somber, slightly tense background music begins. A male narrator, later identified as the Modern Speaker, explains how Goldman Sachs' reputation was "dragged through the mud," a potentially catastrophic situation for an investment bank. The scene transitions to a black-and-white office where two men shake hands, with framed certificates and an 'AFRICAN' poster visible, while the narrator continues, detailing how clients stuck with Goldman Sachs, recognizing their ability to "bounce back from crises" and their focus on "making them money." The Modern Speaker then appears in a modern office, gesturing expressively as he narrates Rubin's successful navigation of Goldman Sachs through the 1987 Black Monday crash and early 1990s legal troubles. Archival photographs follow, showing a smiling gray-haired man in a suit, then standing beside Bill Clinton at a podium, as the narrator states that "Ruben also goes to Washington and becomes a politician." A C-SPAN broadcast from the White House Rose Garden shows the same gray-haired man, now confirmed as Bob Rubin by an older male voice describing him as a "consummate honest broker" and "worthy successor to Lloyd Bentsen." Rubin is seen laughing with another man in a suit at the podium, accompanied by applause and laughter in the audio. The segment concludes with a modern cityscape featuring tall glass buildings and a red traffic light, overlaid with bold yellow text '$2.7B', as the narrator's voice returns, mentioning "2.7" before the audio cuts off, directly linking to the financial figure.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a modern cityscape featuring a tall glass skyscraper, overlaid with bold yellow text reading '$2.7B', as a voiceover explains this represents "dollars in profit," with "a large part of it coming from trading." The scene transitions to a close-up of a document titled 'Our Business Principles', clipped to a yellow folder, with visible text emphasizing client interests and teamwork, while the voiceover states that "for a decade, the firm lived under the principle of putting their clients' interests first." This principle, however, is "gradually shifting because Goldman Sachs' trading business is expanding to a bigger scale, composed mostly of their own money." The camera then pans across a panoramic view of a city skyline at dusk, highlighting the One World Trade Center, before returning to the same skyscraper from a street-level perspective, with a red traffic light in the foreground. The narrative shifts to a man in a blue and white checkered shirt, seated in a modern office, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, serving as the voiceover. He explains that "around that time, Goldman Sachs had about $2 billion of partners' capital," as large yellow text '$2B' appears, followed by '$98B' as he states, "but with $98 billion of total assets," concluding, "So that's a very high leverage." He continues, explaining that "a lot of the partners are retiring and they're leaving with their capital," necessitating "a stable base of capital that can't be pulled out at a moment's notice." The solution, he declares, is "going public." The scene cuts to a dimly lit, vintage office setting where two men in suits sit at a table with green-lidded lamps, one smoking a pipe and reviewing documents, while a third man stands in the background, representing the firm's past. The modern speaker returns, continuing his explanation that "by 1997, Goldman Sachs has two bosses: Jon Corzine and Hank Paulson." A black-and-white street view of Wall Street follows, with classical architecture and American flags, before transitioning to a framed photograph on a brick wall showing two men in shirts and ties—one with a stern expression, the other smiling—overlaid with animated white lines that scan across their faces, suggesting analysis. The camera zooms in on the stern-faced man, as the voiceover describes their arrangement as one that "has worked well for the firm since the 1970s," but warns that "as a public company, there can only be one king." Throughout, a consistent, slightly dramatic instrumental background music plays.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with an aerial shot of a lush green field in Barrington, Illinois, marked by white fences and winding paths, establishing a serene, pastoral setting, accompanied by a somber, instrumental background score. A voice states, "Paulson knows it." The scene transitions to a close-up of a bald man in a dark suit and red patterned tie, wearing glasses, speaking passionately with expressive hand gestures in front of a blurred bookshelf background. His deep, clear voice reflects, "I was a competitive kid. I had a little bit of what my wife ridicules as a gold star mentality. I didn't want to just learn, I wanted to learn, I wanted to get an A." A narrator then explains that "As a devout Christian, Paulson strictly follows a code of conduct in life and business," as black-and-white archival footage of a football player in a jersey with the number 6 appears, followed by a young man in a blue and white checkered shirt speaking in a modern office setting. The narrator continues, "At Goldman Sachs, Hank Paulson was known to have really loyal clients that stick with him through thick and thin," and "I think it's because they see him as an honest broker," while the visuals cut to a black-and-white group photograph of formally dressed men, then to a man in a dark suit and yellow patterned tie standing in a modern office with a cityscape view. The bald man reappears, continuing his speech with animated gestures, his voice stating, "I have never been a career planner. I am ambitious and competitive. But whatever I'm doing at the time seems enormously important to me." A framed photograph on a brick wall shows two men, one bald and one with a beard, both in shirts and ties, smiling. The narrator concludes, "But Hank Paulson doesn't get along with his partner, Corzine. And eventually, the partners of the firm decide to support Paulson as the only senior manager," as the final scene shows a black-and-white conference room where the bald man stands at the head of a long table, addressing a group of seated men in suits, who are taking notes, suggesting a formal business meeting or presentation. The background music maintains a consistent, slightly dramatic tone throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of a framed 'King Henry' magazine cover, prominently displaying a '$3B' overlay, as a male narrator details a firm's success in bringing in "$3 billion pre-tax income for the year." The scene transitions to a wide shot of a modern city skyline, reinforcing the corporate setting, while the narrator explains the partnership is "ready to go public." An ominous musical score begins as the focus shifts to a desk setup featuring a framed photograph of a man in a pinstripe suit, flanked by computer monitors displaying headshots, with the text 'LONG TERM CAPITAL MANAGEMENT' overlaid. The narrator reveals an "unexpected crisis" is about to "ruin their IPO plan." The tension builds with a shot of a man writing complex mathematical equations on a chalkboard, symbolizing the firm's analytical foundation. The narrator then introduces 'LONG TERM CAPITAL MANAGEMENT' as a hedge fund "started by John Meriwether, a famed bond trader at Solomon Brothers, and backed by a group of world-renowned economists." Stan Jonas from FIMAT Brokers, a bald man with a gray beard, is then seen speaking in an office, articulating, "In a moment of tremendous clarity, they realized that two risky positions taken together can effectively eliminate risk itself." The video cuts to a man walking confidently, followed by a modern speaker in a checkered shirt gesturing as the narrator describes the hedge fund's "instant success, making 40% during its first year," and how "many Wall Street firms are competing to become its trading partner." The minute concludes by returning to the desk setup with the framed photograph and monitors, as the narrator mentions the fund operated on "perfect mathematical" principles.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a computer monitor displaying a man in a dark suit and orange tie, standing in a modern interior, while a calm male voice describes "models, designed by genius economists who study the history of the financial market," accompanied by subtle, tense background music. A framed photograph of another man in a suit sits on the desk. The scene transitions to a black screen with the text 'BUT THEY FORGET ONE RULE......' in gold letters, as the tense music continues. A close-up then shows Leo Melamed from the Chicago Mercantile Exchange, wearing glasses and a dark suit, speaking in an office, stating in a distinct, older voice, "That's an old market rule. Uh, the market will test you and do what you don't expect it to do." The video cuts to an aerial daytime view of Moscow, with 'MOSCOW' overlaid, showcasing Red Square and St. Basil’s Cathedral, as the first male voice explains, "In 1998, the so-called Black Swan event happened. Something that their models didn't account for." This shifts to a nighttime shot of the illuminated cathedral, while the voice continues, "A sovereign country defaulting on its debt." The scene changes to an older man with white hair sipping from a wine glass in a dimly lit room, as a calm female voice with a British accent narrates, "August 1998. Something happened no one had considered possible. The biggest country in the world, suddenly and without explanation, refused to pay all its international debts." This is followed by a chaotic, high-angle shot of a crowded trading floor, overlaid with '-$353M', while the first male voice states, "In a single day, LCTM loses $353 million," with the music intensifying. The final scene shows a modern trading desk with multiple monitors, one displaying 'THE OPENING BELL', and a 'Goldman Sachs' sign, as traders work in a dimly lit environment, and the male voice concludes, "After learning about LCTM's trouble, Goldman Sachs immediately starts dumping its own bond positions into the market, accelerating..." The music continues to build.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a man from behind at a high-tech trading desk, surrounded by monitors, with a 'Goldman Sachs' sign visible on the wall, as a male narrator discusses "LCTM's downfall." The narrator continues, explaining how "the Fed comes in and bails out LCTM, and saving Goldman Sachs in the process," while the scene transitions to an exterior shot of the grand Federal Reserve Building. An aerial view of a dense urban skyline, likely Manhattan, then gives way to a grainy archival close-up of Rep. Bernie Sanders, who, with a C-SPAN2 graphic, questions why "the Federal Reserve has organized the three and a half billion dollar bailout for billionaires," and why "Americans should be worried about the gambling practices of the Wall Street elites." The scene transitions through a narrow city street, a bustling modern trading floor with an agitated trader, and a chaotic vintage trading floor, while the narrator reiterates that "LTCM's failure may have almost caused a financial crisis, but for Goldman Sachs, it only matters that Goldman Sachs survives." Tense instrumental music begins to build as a modern glass skyscraper is shown, followed by a contemporary trading floor with 'NYSE' visible on a monitor, and then the iconic New York Stock Exchange facade. The narrator announces, "Now, with the LTCM's crisis resolved, and at the peak of the dot-com bubble, Goldman Sachs goes public," as the music swells dramatically. A news anchor in a studio, with a graphic about 'THE DOW'S NINTH WORST YEAR WAS 1932', reports on "A big debut expected tomorrow for the Goldman Sachs initial public offering." The segment concludes with a black-and-white archival image of men clapping and smiling, an American flag and ticker board in the background, as the narrator confirms, "Goldman Sachs goes public by the end of 1999, selling 11..." before the audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Bear Stearns building entrance, its glass doors reflecting the city, as a narrator explains the "fire sale of Bear Stearns to JP Morgan Chase." The scene transitions to the JPMorgan Chase & Co. building, where a man carries stacked boxes down steps, with the date 'September 16, 2008' overlaid, as the narrator continues, "and the bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers. The entire investment banking sector is on the verge of total ruin." Tense, dramatic music underscores the narrative. The camera then moves to the Goldman Sachs building, its name prominently displayed, as the narrator states that "Having a large exposure to subprime mortgage loans, Goldman Sachs is among the most vulnerable firms on Wall Street." President George W. Bush is then seen speaking at a podium, his expression serious, while the narrator declares, "We are faced with the prospect of a global meltdown." The narrator continues, "Out of options, Goldman Sachs turns to an unlikely savior," as the scene shifts to a black-and-white office where Warren Buffett smiles warmly. The dramatic music intensifies. A CNBC Newsline graphic appears, featuring Buffett and a ticker about his wariness of Lehman's valuations. President George W. Bush's voice is then heard, stating, "I have no idea what the stock market's going to do next month or six months from now. I do know that the American economy over a period of time will do very well and people that want a piece of it will do well." This audio plays over a New York Times article titled 'Buffett Deal at Goldman Seen as a Sign of Confidence,' featuring a photo of Buffett and Goldman Sachs CEO Lloyd Blankfein laughing. The narrator then explains, "Warren Buffett's investment gives Goldman Sachs some breathing room." The final scene shows a dimly lit trading floor where a bald man in a suit speaks urgently on the phone amidst monitors and a chaotic environment, as the narrator concludes, "To recoup from the trading losses, Goldman Sachs not only unloads the mortgage-related assets..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a news anchor, a woman with long blonde hair, in a studio under the CNBC 'CLOSING BELL' banner, reporting on the headline 'GOLDMAN SACHS TO OFFER WEALTH MANAGEMENT FOR THE MASSES'. She clearly states that Goldman Sachs, known for catering to the rich, is now preparing to offer wealth management for the masses. The scene transitions to a stylized animated sequence featuring a family silhouette, a house, and a mountainous backdrop, symbolizing financial security and growth. A calm female voice explains that 'Investing can be an important part of creating the life you want,' leading to the reveal of the 'Marcus: by Goldman Sachs' logo, as a male voice introduces it as 'Marcus Invest, an automated way to invest from Goldman Sachs.' A gentle, uplifting background music track plays throughout this segment, becoming more ambient. The narrative then shifts to real-world footage of a modern urban environment, showing pedestrians walking near a sleek glass building with the address '200 WEST STREET' visible above the entrance, suggesting a Goldman Sachs office. The camera pans across the building’s exterior, capturing its illuminated glass facade and American flags waving. Over this, a deep, authoritative male voice declares, 'It is now 2022, and the world has changed. But for Goldman Sachs, it's still the same hungry, opportunistic company.' An older-sounding male voice with a slightly gravelly tone then praises the firm, stating, 'They have got the most formidable internal culture of commitment and teamwork of any organization in finance in the history of the world.' The segment concludes with a dramatic, darkly lit animated sequence featuring a large, translucent blue octopus with textured skin, its tentacles moving through a miniature cityscape. The deep, authoritative male voice asserts, 'In a game of the survival of the fittest, Goldman Sachs has managed to outlast the majority.' The golden text 'GOLDMAN SACHS' emerges in front of the octopus, symbolizing the firm’s pervasive influence. The atmosphere is moody and cinematic, with fog and low lighting. The older-sounding male voice then adds, 'It's a firm that is highly disciplined and efficient for one thing... Profit.' The ambient music becomes more intense and dramatic, with a low, sustained hum and subtle percussive elements, ending with a final, resonant sound.
